---
title: "BEAR"
subtitle: "Benchmarks of Empirical Accuracy in Research"
---



::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
## Problem

Quantitative metascience drives debates about important issues about research standards.
Most of the crucial contributions in this branch of metascience have been 
based on analysing individual datasets, often painstakingly constructed by researchers.
Thanks to their work we now have better understanding of publication bias, p-hacking, 
reporting practices, pre-registration, significance rates, and more. 
But many canoninical findings of metascience are based on individual datasets,
each constructed in a specific way. How generalisable are these findings?

:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
## Solution

BEAR is a open-source database for metascience exploration
with 25 datasets across many scientific fields and millions of data points. 
BEAR does not generate any new source data; it repackages publicly available datasets 
into a common structure to make research on replication, exchangeability, 
meta-analysis, and other questions about empirical research easier and more generalisable.
:::
:::


::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
To download latest version of data, go to
[GitHub Releases](https://github.com/wwiecek/BEAR/releases). 
or you can follow [download instructions](getting-started.qmd) for command line.
:::

::: {.grid}
::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
## Datasets

BEAR combines curated registries of studies, random collections of articles in 
different disciplines, sets of meta-analyses, and direct replication projects.
These are purposefully heterogeneous datasets of varying quality.
The dataset covers many disciplines: econonmics, political science, biomedicine, 
clinical trials, ecology, evolution, education, neuroscience, and more. 
Head to [datasets section](datasets.qmd) to read about individual datasets.
:::

::: {.g-col-12 .g-col-md-6}
## What to do with these data

At a minimum BEAR contains z-values with clearly labelled identifiers for studies
and meta-analyses. Where available, it also includes effect sizes, standard errors, sample sizes,
methods (e.g. parallel RCT), measures (e.g. standardised mean difference, odds ratio),
and additional covariates (e.g. year, pre-registration status, phase of clinical trial,
journal, discipline).

An optional model of signal-to-noise ratios (based on van Zwet et al) 
is also inlcuded as part of the repository, to quantify basic metascientific parameters
like selection, assurance, and replicability of findings. Users are encouraged 
to fit their own models.
:::
:::


![](assets/bear_banner.png){.bear-banner fig-alt="BEAR banner"}


## Links

- [Get data](getting-started.qmd) for a minimal R example.
- [Datasets](datasets.qmd) for the current dataset index, basic results, and individual
  dataset descriptions.
- [Reproduce](reproduce.qmd) for more complete workflow of rebuilding the database
  from individual data sources and modelling.
- [GitHub repository](https://github.com/wwiecek/BEAR) to access code and dataset 
  directly.
